sentences.text: Koszeg.
Kojak is dead.
He died a year ago.
He was my favorite.
All the Hungarians liked or disliked the Jew was coming without anything on the train.
And he wants to cause him trouble.
He was hoping that somebody's going to shoot me.
Well, how are we going?
The hair is OK?
Good.
I enjoy it.
No that was in a combination concentration camp.
So we were taken, but before we were taken, we have been put on a-- a-- a cattle wagon.
There were about 80 people in the wagon, which would have been comfortable for about 20, maybe less.
And, before we-- There were safe houses in Budapest.
Now there was one by the Vatican, and one by the Swiss, and the third one was the real one by the Swiss.
It was Wallenstein-- Wallenstein.
Wallenberg, he wasn't Jewish.

Wallenberg and he has given out various documents for Jews, and if somebody was taken by the Hungarian police or this extreme rightist, and he has shown this Wallenberg document, he was either let go or he was shot.
So that was an easy choice.
But at least somebody had a chance.
Maybe he is going to be let go.
And there were some apartment buildings, which were rented by the Swedish Embassy where Jews were safe.
That's why they called it the safe house.

Now there was a man sitting in the dark with a light beaming from behind him, and he was yelling while we were putting in the wagon, if you have a Swedish-- Swedish papers, Swedish pass, then just come to me, and even if yours was destroyed.
And he had a book in front of him with all the names who were-- who had--
Outside the wagon, yeah.
The what?
No, it was on the side track someplace.
It wasn't in this-- they didn't make much propaganda, how they are treating Jews.
It was a German train.
No, it was a-- listen, there was no where to escape because the Hungarians hated Jews so badly, they would have killed a Jew immediately.
I mean there was just no way.
And so there were, of course, some Jews who escaped.
There was a-- when we were in Budapest at that time, and we were traveling by truck from one part of the Danube to the other part, we have seen dead bodies on the street.
Those dead bodies weren't there because they committed suicide, they were there because they were shot.
So you didn't have to do anything.
Now, this bread was already moldy-- not moldy a little bit, moldy all over.
It was in a kind of a plastic bag.
No it wasn't a plastic bag, it was just moldy.
And we got it.
Now at the beginning, we were trying to scrape off the mold.
It was a greyish stuff, and we were eating it.
And later on, when we were very hungry, we didn't care mold or not, we ate the whole thing because we didn't get it too often.
So then we were taken to Koszeg--
Hmm?
I arrived in, I think, end of November.
Or the first week in December.
I remember the weather was very mild at that time.
It was like the weather what we had this year in December, and it was bright and sunny.
It wasn't very warm, but it was there.
So the first time we were accommodated in-- in a building, in a beer factory's building.
And then later on, after a week or two, there were some Germans came to watch over us, and we had to evacuate it.
And we were about, I would say, 12 to 14 in one of those huts, which only was good only for about four people.
But the reason we survived is because we were so many of us, and we were in-- we were lying in that hut as if, as sardines.
Like the head was at the end and the feet was in the middle.
So that's the way we were keeping warm.
Again, I was in a sleeping bag, and I had a very good protections.
I didn't-- the sleeping bag was solidly protecting me, and the other people had one of those things, among other covers--
Blankets.
Blankets we were sleeping on or-- or-- or-- or something else.
Now anyway, we were here, and we went out to dig ditches.
They were tank traps.
It means that the Russians against who couldn't be stopped when they were crossing over the car paths about 2,000 meters high-- which is about 2,000/6,000 feet high.
They were supposed to be stop at the flat land between the Austrian and Hungarian border, so we were digging and digging.
Now it went OK in December, but in January it became hard.
The land, the ground froze over, and it was just impossible to dig.
Now the Germans knew it, but they were still yelling at us.
Move!
Move!
And tempo!
Tempo!
And they insisted we should-- by that time we had axes, but the axes didn't work either because it was just digging into solid cement, or solid rock, or solid anything.
But we went out, and then we pretended that we are digging, so we were moving.
And after a while, when the weather became colder and colder, and my shoe wasn't warm enough, my big toe and the small toe started to freeze over.
It turned black or dark blue.
And I said, well, if it's going to go on like this, I'm going to lose my toes.
And if I lose my toes I'm going to lose my life.
And I decided I'm going to report it that I cannot go out to work anymore.
So since it was a combination of concentration camp and forced labor, there was such a thing that I have decided that I'm not going to go out, so I was staying in, and I was staying in all winter.
Now to tell you how life was in-- in that camp, I have to tell you there was a huge big container which one was where the water was kept.
Now the container was as big as I would say as a small room, and it was filled with water.
And this water was serving for washing yourself, washing your dish, and washing-- and drinking of it.
So the dish was, of course, dirty, and we didn't wash our hands because we needed the water to drink.
And the food which they have given us, it was two times.
Once, in the morning, it was some hot-- they called it a coffee.
And I-- I said, as my wife mentioned it to me sometime, it was like black coal instead of coffee.
And then in the evening, we got another thing which they called the soup.
Soup which one where there was a floating all sorts of things among other maybe a piece of noodle or two, and that was keeping us alive, plus one slice of bread, one solitary slice with water.
We have, of course-- it was prepared by the kitchen.
Now the people who were working in the kitchen, they had other food to eat.
Only thing is that didn't get down to us.
Now the people who were in the kitchen were Jews.
Now, of course, the Jews were keeping the food, a good part of the food for themselves because they were very well fed, and we were getting slimmer and slimmer.
It had an advantage.
There were some people who were diabetic, very, very-- well, and they were worried what is going to happen to them when their insulin, or whatever preparation they were taking, is going to run out.
They didn't have to worry about it.
The diabetics became as same as anybody because the minute they lost the weight, they lost the cholesterol, they lost everything what causes diabetic.
And when they have returned, whoever returned to Hungary, they were healthy until they started to eat again.
So there were some people who are benefiting by being in a concentration camp.
Now the worst thing was when somebody got diarrhea.
Once you got the diarrhea, you died because there was nothing to stop the diarrhea.
There were some doctors with us who have taken bones.
You know, the kitchen had bones.
So if the kitchen had bones, they must have had some meat on the bones, but we never got the meat part of the bones.
They were burnt to coal.
And then when it was burnt to the coal, they were hammering them to-- to be powdery, and this was given to people who got diarrhea.
But it didn't have-- one of the doctors also got diarrhea, and he just died just as the rest-- some of the other people in the camp.
Now we have been there until April, and they, at the end, they decided that we are going to be transported further when the Russians came.
And the camp was divided into three different parts.
Now there were people who went out regularly to work, and there were the people who didn't have much trouble anymore, as my feet became all right.
And there was the third part who couldn't walk at all, and they decided that they are going to claim that they won't be able to walk, and that was an easy solution for them.
They were put into three huts full of people, and they were covered with blankets, and they were told that the lice is going to be killed, all the varmints.
And they really prepared those three huts really well, and there was a German man coming with cyanide, and the cyanide was pumped into those three huts.
And the people just died.
Except one survived.
And that man started to yell, let me out, let me out, let me out.
And he was yelling for two solid days.
Nobody let him out.
The German was standing in front of the hut.
And eventually, after two months-- after two days, they let him out and shot him.
So that was a solution for everything.
I remember one evening, we woke up because somebody was yelling-- it was before the end.
And he said, don't shoot me, don't shoot me.
So we didn't know what happened.
And it was worth in black market, three pengos, and he sold it for a 100 pengo.
So imagine what profit she was making.
But she has taken the chance.
And then when there was another occasion.
There were other women coming, and they were selling-- they were making 97 pengos on-- on one bread.
Now it was highway robbery.
And the Germans saw it happen, but they didn't interfere.
Now, I just wanted to say how nicely the Hungarians treated us.
Nobody have-- so we went-- those three groups I was talking about.
The first one left a few days before, and we were left and that we have seen what happened to those people who decide that they cannot walk.
Now the first group went over to Austria.
And they were digging a huge, big grave.
Now not a single grave, a huge big grave.
Now those graves were dug for us, like for me and the other ones, who decide that they are not going to walk.
And we were put on a train.
And with a train, we went from Koszeg to Szombathely and through the Hungarian-Austrian border.
And there everybody was told to get off the train.
And we were still too many for the Germans.
Seemingly, the grave wasn't large enough.
And we were asked again if there is somebody who wants rather get on the flat cart or flat carriage, horse-drawn carriage.
No, I didn't know it yet.
I knew it later because we were reunited with that group which was digging the grave.
So there were some people.
They were getting on a horse-drawn carriage, and they were carried to the grave, which was quite a distance from us, and they were shot dead.
Now we know it because the people who were digging and burying the bodies were rejoining us, and they were telling us this story.
Now we started to walk.
This is what I would call a death march from the Hungarian border to Mauthausen to Gunskirchen.
Now, this is, I would say we were walking three weeks.
As we started to walk, my clothing wasn't in very good shape, so I have seen their Hungarian soldier's uniform.
So I have taken mine off, and I have put on the soldier's uniform.
But it wasn't against the law.
I mean, people were carrying all sorts of uniforms.
And anyway, there was no law.
If somebody-- if the German objected to it-- by that time, we were given over to the Germans.
There was no more Hungarian guards left.
From then on, I was walking in a uniform.
Now the uniform was good, if I would have known where to go.
I could have gone anywhere because the uniform was a Hungarian uniform.
It's a rucksack.
And I filled that one up, and then all of a sudden, lo and behold, the doors open up in the house across from there, and that was an SS officer coming out.
I said well, now or never.
And I saw that he had a large bread in his head-- in his hand, not head, hand.
I don't know what he had in the head.
But he was really friendly and gave it to me.
I said, gee whiz, if all the SS would be like you, it would be terrific.
He had no idea that I'm Jewish, but I had an idea that I'm Jewish.
And I also knew, if I'm going to go down to Todd, and there is going to be another Hungarian there, and I'm circumcised-- in case I'm going to go to sleep-- I'm not going to survive the morning.
So I said, thank you very much, and I went back to the rest of the kids and-- kids.
There was a man I've always-- everybody had to have somebody, at least another man with whom you were in a buddy relation so you could help each other.
So I was giving some of the apples to him and half of the bread.
We have put the apple on a stick, and we have been frying it on-- on-- on-- on-- on on an open fire, and it was very, very sweet.
It lasted for about two or three days, and after that there was, again, nothing.
But I have eaten the lucerne.
It was very sweet.
I don't know if it would be sweet today, but it was very sweet at that time.
And then they have thrown another apple down, and the Jews were killing each other.
They were milling, you know, and they were stepping on each other.
So when-- of course, I didn't participate in this business because I was not interested to kill or trample on another Jew.
Maybe, if maybe you could get an apple.
But there were other people who were brothers or very good friends, they were looking the position.
One said I want to be 10-- one, two, three-- and there's going to be there.
If I die, he's going to die.
If I survive, he's going to survive.
The other one said, I want to be five, and he's going to be the tenth.
It means that one of us is going to survive.
There were all sorts of combination, and they were running like crazy.
They were changing, and they said, let me in, let me-- I said, I'll let you in, I don't care.
I mean, who knows what is going to help in the decimation.
Now we are getting from [NON-ENGLISH] to [NON-ENGLISH].
And then we went through the mountains, through Eisenerz to Hieflau, and-- and there was one point about--
